role_summary: Founding member of the Howard University Nonviolent Action Group (NAG); Freedom Rider on the May 1961 Montgomery-to-Jackson continuation ride; SNCC field secretary doing voter registration in McComb, Mississippi and Baton Rouge, Louisiana.
ai_reading: The embedding reading that the cultural record would not predict sits on the Nonviolence as Theology versus Armed Self-Defense axis: Diamond's testimony places at +0.24 toward the Armed Self-Defense pole, away from the theological-nonviolence framing that the Freedom Rider label culturally implies. That position is not an artifact; it tracks what he says on the record, that he believed nonviolence as a tactic rather than a philosophy because the police and the army held the guns. On the other four concept axes the vector is closer to center (his largest other reading is +0.34 toward Collective Discipline, with Southern Struggle at -0.19 and the sacred-secular and tactical-strategic axes both inside +/-0.17), so the self-defense lean is the one axis where the embedding takes a clear side. His top semantic neighbors by shared-passage count are Harold K. Brown (entry 55, count 111), C. T. Vivian (entry 134, count 83), Robert L. Carter (entry 108, count 82), Joan Trumpauer Mulholland (entry 60, count 75), and Judge D'Army Bailey (entry 26, count 64), with Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 60) just behind. The cluster is heterogeneous, spanning SCLC (Vivian), the NAACP Legal Defense lawyer Carter, a fellow Freedom Rider (Mulholland), and Brown, rather than concentrating in Diamond's Howard NAG and SNCC affiliation. The influence graph adds a corroborating cross-reference: Diamond is named in this corpus by D'Army Bailey (entry 26), Timothy Jenkins (entry 120), and the Ladner sisters (entry 75), three voices spread across the Baton Rouge campus protest, the Howard student-government circle, and the Mississippi field, which is the same cross-setting spread the neighbor list shows.
biographical_paragraph: Dion Diamond enrolled at Howard University in September 1959 as a physics major and, as a freshman class president, was among the founders of the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG), the campus affiliate of SNCC that organized sit-ins and pickets across Washington, D.C. and the adjacent counties of Virginia and Maryland [src: 1, 2]. NAG's first sit-in, in June 1960 at the Drug Fair and Peoples Drug counters in Arlington, Virginia, was followed within two weeks by desegregation commitments across Arlington and Fairfax counties and Alexandria, and by a summer-long picket of the segregated Glen Echo amusement park in Maryland [src: 2, 3]. He had been born February 7, 1941 in Petersburg, Virginia, where he spent his first eighteen years [src: 2, 4]. The Freedom Rides nearly collapsed after the May 14, 1961 firebombing of a Greyhound bus outside Anniston, Alabama; Diamond joined a second wave of buses that carried the campaign forward on the Montgomery-to-Jackson, Mississippi leg, and he was jailed at Parchman, the Mississippi State Penitentiary [src: 1, 3]. As a SNCC field secretary he did voter-registration work in McComb, Mississippi, sharing a house there with Robert Parris Moses, and in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, where he was charged with criminal anarchy for organizing a student boycott at Southern University; across his civil rights activity he was arrested about thirty times [src: 1, 3]. He transferred to the University of Wisconsin in August 1963 and switched his major from physics to history, and he later earned a graduate degree at Harvard in race and ethnic relations [src: 1, 3]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 27 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Dion Diamond
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked whether nonviolence was a philosophy or a tactic, Diamond drew the line on practical grounds:
interview_snippets.quote: I don't believe it in it as a philosophy. My reasoning is the police and the army have the guns and the ammunition that we had nothing.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dion Diamond
interview_snippets.lead_in: Moved through Mississippi jails after the Freedom Ride arrest, he found himself sharing a death-row cell at the state penitentiary:
interview_snippets.quote: I shared a cell with James Farmer, who was then the director of CORE.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dion Diamond
interview_snippets.lead_in: On voter-registration assignment in McComb, sleeping in a room beside a low lattice window, he described the night the danger turned literal:
interview_snippets.quote: But a shotgun blast came straight through the window, passed over my body.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dion Diamond
interview_snippets.lead_in: Arrested in Baton Rouge for organizing a student boycott at Southern University, he recounted the charge and conceded its logic:
interview_snippets.quote: The main charge they got me on was criminal anarchy. They said I was attempting to overthrow the state of Louisiana. And in fact, that's true. I mean, I was.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dion Diamond
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reflecting on why he tells the history to young audiences, he reached for an inscription he had read on a building downtown:
interview_snippets.quote: But somewhere downtown on one of the buildings, there's this Shakespearean phrase, what is past is prologue. I think that's true. If you don't know your history, you should. You can very easily revert to what existed previously.

interview_snippets.speaker: Timothy Jenkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: Timothy Jenkins, then moving from undergraduate to graduate at Howard, places Diamond in the incoming class that became the core of NAG:
interview_snippets.quote: Stokely Carmichael, Courtland Cox, Dion Diamond, Jean Wheeler, and a number of other students had just been admitted as freshmen at Howard.

interview_snippets.speaker: Judge D'Army Bailey
interview_snippets.lead_in: D'Army Bailey, a Southern University student in Baton Rouge during the 1961-62 protests, recalls who arrived to push the campaign forward:
interview_snippets.quote: one of the leaders that had gotten us stirred up in this second wave of protest was a young man by the name of Dion Diamond who had come to Baton Rouge, but Dion was a SNCC organizer that came to the Southern campus.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dorie Ann Ladner and Joyce Ann Ladner
interview_snippets.lead_in: A Ladner sister remembers the meeting in a Masonic Temple above Medgar Evers's office where the SNCC organizers gathered with local Mississippi youth:
interview_snippets.quote: Dion Diamond, all these young people were there, and it's like, oh my God, I've been looking for you since I was 14 years old.

movement_context: The May 1961 Freedom Rides were organized by the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) under James Farmer's direction, with the first set of thirteen riders (six white, seven Black) leaving Washington, D.C. on May 4, 1961 in two integrated buses bound for New Orleans; the goal was testing the December 5, 1960 Boynton v. Virginia Supreme Court ruling that interstate-bus terminal segregation violated the Interstate Commerce Act's anti-discrimination provisions [src: 5, 7]. Diamond did not ride the original buses; he joined a second wave that carried the campaign forward after the original CORE riders were stopped by the May 14, 1961 Anniston firebombing of the Greyhound bus and the parallel Trailways bus beatings [src: 5, 3]. The continuation was sustained by Nashville student riders who reached Birmingham on May 17, and it brought Diamond onto the Montgomery-to-Jackson, Mississippi leg; in his own account, his bus was escorted into Jackson under National Guard and state-police convoy because a deal had been struck that the riders would not be beaten but would be arrested on arrival [src: 1, 5]. By the end of the summer 1961 ride campaign, more than four hundred Freedom Riders had been arrested in Jackson alone; the Interstate Commerce Commission's November 1, 1961 ruling banning bus-terminal segregation was the federal-regulatory outcome the campaign produced [src: 5]. The Howard NAG Diamond helped found was a pipeline through which the Howard cohort (Stokely Carmichael, Courtland Cox, Charles Cobb, Muriel Tillinghast, Jean Wheeler, among others) entered SNCC's organizing cadre across 1960-1963; Timothy Jenkins (entry 120) names Diamond among the freshmen 'stalwarts in the founding of SNCC' in that cohort [src: 2, 6].

gallery.alt: Birmingham News front page coverage of the May 14, 1961 Anniston Freedom Rider attacks
gallery.caption: Theodore Gaffney's photograph of the Anniston Greyhound bus attack as it appeared on the front page of the Birmingham News, Sunday May 14, 1961. The bus had been firebombed by a Ku Klux Klan mob on U.S. Highway 78 outside Anniston; Diamond joined the SNCC-organized continuation rides after the original CORE riders were stopped by this attack and the parallel Trailways bus beating in Birmingham later the same day.
